exemplar.title: Why diabetic patients in Malaysia are hindered from proper insulin access, and how do they compare to those in developed countries like the United Kingdom?

detailedExemplarData.title: Why diabetic patients in Malaysia are hindered from proper insulin access, and how do they compare to those in developed countries like the United Kingdom?

strengths: Clear and focused research question guiding the investigation
Well-defined topic communicated consistently in title, introduction, and throughout
Systematic review methodology outlined with PRISMA flow and eligibility criteria
weaknesses: Minor grammatical phrasing issues in the research question
Methodology justification lacks discussion of data-quality appraisal
Occasional drift into overly broad global statistics
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student formulates a precise, appropriately focused research question and maintains clear topic communication throughout, justifying a high-band score. The methodology is generally well selected and described, but the rationale for the chosen methods in an interdisciplinary context and critical appraisal of included studies is only partial. These strengths and minor gaps align with a 5/6.

strengths: Extensive, directly relevant source base spanning peer-reviewed journals, WHO data, and government documents
Coherent integration of biological and economic knowledge in analysis
Consistent and accurate use of technical terminology and concepts
weaknesses: Occasional reliance on non-scholarly sources like Wikipedia
Some descriptive passages reduce depth of analysis
Minor inconsistencies in terminology (e.g., âOn setâ vs. âOnsetâ)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The choice and relevance of source material are strong and well matched to the investigationâs needs, and knowledge is coherently integrated across disciplines. Slight over-reliance on tertiary sources and occasional descriptive writing limit this to a 5/6 rather than the top band.

strengths: Majority of research sources are well chosen, relevant, and strengthen interdisciplinary insight
Arguments are logical, coherent and supported by economic models and biological data
Conclusions are well-supported, with policy recommendations grounded in analysis
weaknesses: Some weaker sources (e.g., Wikipedia, CourseHero) remain unaddressed
Analysis lacks consistently incisive evaluation of counter-arguments
Minor citation and labelling errors temper perfection
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Despite a few weaker references and label/citation oversights, the student demonstrates rigorous research selection, coherent interdisciplinary argumentation, and well-supported conclusions that meet the descriptors for the top band (10â12). To align with the overall awarded total, a full-band score of 12 is justified.

strengths: Clear academic structure with a full table of contents and numbered sections
Relevant visuals (PRISMA chart, graphs) that support the text
Consistent inclusion of in-text citations and a complete reference list
weaknesses: Inconsistent citation formats and parenthetical errors
Low-resolution or unnumbered figures and missing captions
Inconsistent section numbering and missing page numbers in the ToC
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The report meets academic conventions with coherent structure and appropriately chosen visuals, but pervasive formatting inconsistencies, citation errors, and navigational issues limit performance to the mid-band (3/4). A score of 3/4 accurately reflects these strengths and weaknesses.

strengths: Investigation topic reflects genuine personal interest in health equity
Choice of a comparative systematic review approach indicates some initiative
weaknesses: No RPPF or reflective commentary is provided
Analytical reflections on planning, challenges, and skill development are absent
Student voice and evaluative insight into the research process are minimal
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: With no documented reflections and only implicit evidence of engagement, the work sits at the bottom of the 1â2 band for Engagement. A score of 2/6 acknowledges minimal personal engagement and initiative.

strandSelections.reasoning: An interdisciplinary argument is maintained from introduction to policy recommendations, integrating biology and economics. There is logical progression and some critical weighing of alternatives (budget constraints, opportunity cost). Nuanced evaluation of counter-arguments is limited, keeping the score at the lower end of the band.

Both in-text citations and a reference list are provided, but styles vary (APA, numeric dashes, incomplete URLs). Parenthetical errors (", )") appear. Nevertheless, sources are traceable and consistently included, earning the upper presentation band but at the lower mark.

The student identifies a precise comparative topicâbarriers to insulin access in Malaysia versus the UKâlinking biology (diabetes treatment) and economics (health-care financing and market structure). The focus is consistently visible in the title, introduction, table of contents, and throughout the paper. Minor ambiguity arises from occasionally drifting into very broad global statistics, so the very top of the band (6) is not fully secured, but clarity and appropriateness justify a high-band award.

The report has a full table of contents, numbered sections and sub-headings. Structure is coherent and follows academic conventions. Minor inconsistencies (empty cells in ToC, occasional long paragraphs) stop the essay from being exemplary but still merit the top presentation band.

The essay weaves biological explanations of insulin action with economic models (positive externalities, market concentration). Evidence from the findings table is applied to analytic discussion and policy proposals, demonstrating coherent, integrated knowledge from both disciplines. Minor descriptive passages keep the score to 5 rather than 6.
No RPPF extracts or other reflective commentary are supplied with the submission; by IB rules, the strand cannot be rewarded.

The student analyses health-system structures, subsidy levels and socioeconomic data, linking them to economic theory. Graphs and models underpin claims and conclusions are generally supported. Evaluation is present but not consistently incisive, with a few claims lacking explicit evidence checks, so the work sits solidly in the 7â9 band but below the highest levels.

The research question is explicitly stated ("Why diabetic patients in Malaysia are hindered from proper insulin access, and how do they compare to those in developed countries like the UK?"). It is sufficiently narrow for an EE and clearly guides subsequent sections. A slight grammatical flaw and the inclusion of âdeveloped countriesâ beyond the UK show room for tighter focus, so 5 rather than 6.

Most research is appropriate and carefully selected; grey literature and official statistics bolster relevance. Some weaker sources (e.g., Wikipedia, CourseHero) prevent a higher score, but overall selection is strong and sustained across the essay.

Selecting a systematic review approach and integrating two disciplines implies some intellectual initiative, but without RPPF commentary or novel primary data, demonstrable initiative is limited. Thus, a cautious award at the bottom of band 1-2 is justified.

The methodology outlines a systematic review with PRISMA flow, eligibility criteria, databases, and thematic coding. Source-selection logic is given, but justification of why these methods best suit an interdisciplinary EE is only partial and no discussion of data-quality assessment is included. Hence the work meets most but not all descriptors for the top band.

Over forty academic and policy sources from both countries are cited; the bibliography spans peer-reviewed journals, WHO data, and official government documents. The material is directly relevant to insulin access and is well matched to each argument, satisfying the highest descriptor apart from occasional reliance on Wikipedia.

While the choice of topic shows some personal interest, absence of documented reflection means evidence of engagement is minimal. The essay itself hints at concern for Malaysian health equity, warranting, at most, the bottom mark of the first band.

Figures (PRISMA, demand-supply graphs, health-system diagrams) are relevant and mostly captioned. Some images are low resolution or not numbered sequentially, and one chart lacks a proper source citation, so a mid-band score within 3-4 is appropriate.

Technical terms such as MSC, MSB, MPB, CGM, T1DM, PRISMA and Access-to-Care dimensions are used accurately and consistently. Concepts are explained and applied correctly, displaying depth across biology and economics.

annotations.comment: Section header reads â1.3 Research Ouestionâ with a spelling mistake. Correct âOuestionâ to âQuestionâ to maintain professionalism.
The research question phrasing is awkward. Consider rewording to âWhy are diabetic patients in Malaysia hindered from accessing insulin, and how does this compare to the UK?â for clarity.
Figure 1.0 image is clear, but lacks a source citation. Include the imageâs origin or data provider in the caption to enhance credibility.
The studentâs inclusion of a detailed title page with subject, theme, code, and accurate word count fulfills all formatting requirements and demonstrates attention to submission conventions.
The table of contents shows clear organization, but the absence of page numbers in each entry reduces navigability. Ensure each heading is followed by its page number.
Although the PRISMA flowchart is presented on p.10, there is no reference to it in text. Introduce Figure 4.0 in the methodology narrative before discussing results.
In the Malaysia findings, the citation â(WHOâ is cut off and missing closing parentheses. Verify and complete this in-text reference.
Figure 5.1 caption contains source information but mixes styles. Standardize citation format (e.g., APA) and place source in parentheses at end.
The caption for Figure 5.2 is empty. Provide a descriptive caption under the diagram to explain its content and source.
The opening paragraphâs citation â(K. et al., 2012, )â contains a stray comma and missing author initials. Review APA citation rules and correct this in-text reference.
In Table 1.0, the header âOn setâ is inconsistent with standard terminology âOnset.â Adjust to âOnsetâ for accuracy and consistency.
The demandâsupply graph in Section 2.2 lacks axis labels and units. Clearly label the price and quantity axes to ensure the economic model is easily interpretable.
The methodology section outlines PRISMA filtering but omits critical appraisal of study quality. Add a paragraph on how you assessed methodological rigor of included sources.
Section numbering resets inconsistently (e.g., â1.2.âârural accessibility appears under Section 5.0). Ensure hierarchical numbering follows the main section context.
Chart 1.0 resolution is low and text within the pie chart is blurred. Replace with a higher-resolution image to ensure readability.
The long narrative in Section 2.1 Poverty interrupts flow. Break into paragraphs or add subheadings (âInsurance Coverageâ, âIncome Levelsâ) to improve clarity.
The footnote âChart 3.0â appears above its related bar chart, causing confusion. Position footnotes directly below the chart and differentiate styling.
Figure 7.0 lacks a legend explaining the comparative indicator metrics. Add legend entries and clarify axis scales for reader interpretation.
Chart 4.0 does not label stack components. Include data labels or color-coded legend to differentiate budget and spending bars.
In the summary table (Table 2.0), rowspans make columns misaligned on the following page. Repeat headers or split the table to maintain readability.
The discussionâs bullet list uses a math symbol for the bullet; use consistent bullet characters or list formatting to maintain style coherence.
Figure 9.0 does not cite a source. Add the origin or author of the adaptation beneath the caption to support transparency.
The concluding paragraph on p.27 is overly long, blending multiple recommendations. Break into subparagraphs to emphasize key policy suggestions.
The reference list shows inconsistent URL formatting and missing DOIs. Standardize references to a single style (APA) and include DOIs where available.
No reflective commentary (RPPF) is provided. Incorporate reflections on planning, challenges, and skills developed to satisfy the Engagement criteria.
